title: Loose vs Strict Equality
description: Predict the output and explain why each comparison produces its result.
expectedOutput:
true
false
true
false
true
true
solution:
== performs type coercion; === checks both type and value without coercion.
- 5 == '5': true (string '5' coerces to number 5)
- 5 === '5': false (different types)
- null == undefined: true (special case in loose equality)
- null === undefined: false (different types)
- 0 == false: true (false coerces to 0)
- '' == false: true ('' coerces to 0, false coerces to 0)
explanation: The == operator triggers type coercion before comparison. If types differ, JavaScript converts them using ToNumber(), ToString(), or ToBoolean() rules. The === operator never coerces types.
hints: Remember the 6 falsy values: false, 0, '', null, undefined, NaN
null == undefined is a special case — they're loosely equal to each other only
When comparing with ==, if one operand is a boolean, convert it to a number first

gradingCriteria.explanationDepth: Should explain coercion rules and why null/undefined are special

title: The 6 Falsy Values
description: Which of these will execute their console.log? Explain which are truthy and which are falsy.
solution: Only A, B, and H log. The 6 falsy values are: false, 0, '', null, undefined, NaN. Everything else is truthy, including empty arrays [], empty objects {}, and the string 'false'.
explanation: JavaScript has exactly 6 falsy values. Empty arrays and objects are truthy because they're complex types that exist as objects. The string 'false' is truthy because it's a non-empty string.
hints: Arrays and objects are always truthy, even when empty
The string 'false' is different from the boolean false

gradingCriteria.answerAccuracy: Must correctly identify A, B, H as logging
gradingCriteria.explanationDepth: Should list all 6 falsy values and explain why arrays/objects are truthy

title: The Plus Operator & Concatenation
description: Predict the output and explain why. Pay attention to when + means concatenation vs addition.
solution:
The + operator behaves differently from other operators:
- With at least one string: concatenation (converts both to strings)
- With only numbers: addition

Other operators (-, *, /) always force numeric coercion:
- '2' - 1 = 1 (string converts to number)
- 'hello' - 5 = NaN (can't convert 'hello' to number)

Array/object coercion:
- [].toString() = '' (empty string)
- {}.toString() = '[object Object]'
- [] + [] = '' + '' = ''
- [] + {} = '' + '[object Object]' = '[object Object]'
explanation: The + operator has special coercion rules. It prioritizes string concatenation. All other arithmetic operators force numeric coercion. When strings can't convert to numbers, the result is NaN.
hints: The + operator is unique — it does concatenation if ANY operand is a string
All other operators (-, *, /) force numeric coercion

gradingCriteria.explanationDepth: Should explain why + behaves differently and why 'hello' - 5 = NaN

title: Complex Equality Comparisons
description: Predict each comparison and explain the coercion path. These are tricky!
expectedOutput:
true
false
true
false
true
false
false
false
solution:
When comparing array/object with boolean using ==:
- [] == false: [] → '' → 0, false → 0, so 0 == 0 = true
- [] == true: [] → '' → 0, true → 1, so 0 == 1 = false
- [1] == true: [1] → '1' → 1, true → 1, so 1 == 1 = true
- [1,2] == true: [1,2] → '1,2' → NaN, true → 1, so NaN == 1 = false
- '1' == true: '1' → 1, true → 1, so 1 == 1 = true
- 2 == true: 2 → 2, true → 1, so 2 == 1 = false
- null == 0: null is ONLY loosely equal to undefined, not 0
- undefined == 0: undefined is ONLY loosely equal to null, not 0
explanation: When comparing different types, JavaScript coerces to numbers. Booleans convert to 0 or 1. Arrays convert to their string representation first, then to numbers. null and undefined are special — they're only loosely equal to each other.
hints: When array converts to string with multiple elements, the result might be NaN
null and undefined are NOT loosely equal to 0 or any falsy value

gradingCriteria.explanationDepth: Should show the coercion path for each comparison, especially null/undefined cases

title: Object Coercion with valueOf and toString
description: Predict the output when objects with custom valueOf and toString methods are coerced.
solution:
JavaScript calls valueOf() first for numeric coercion, then toString() if valueOf returns an object.

- obj1 + 0: valueOf() returns 42, so 42 + 0 = 42
- obj2 + 0: valueOf() returns object, so calls toString() → 'hello', then 'hello' + 0 = 'hello0'
- obj3 + 0: valueOf() returns 10, so 10 + 0 = 10 (toString() is not called)
- String(obj2): Calls toString() directly → 'hello'
- Number(obj1): Calls valueOf() → 42
- obj1 == 42: valueOf() returns 42, so 42 == 42 = true
- obj3 == '5': valueOf() returns 10, so 10 == '5' → 10 == 5 = false (not '5')
explanation: When coercing objects to primitives, JavaScript follows a preference order: valueOf() is tried first for numeric contexts, toString() for string contexts. If valueOf() returns a primitive, it's used; otherwise toString() is called. The key insight is that valueOf() has priority in numeric operations, but String() explicitly calls toString().
hints: valueOf() is preferred in numeric contexts (arithmetic, comparison)
toString() is called as a fallback if valueOf() returns an object
String() explicitly calls toString() without trying valueOf() first
In obj3 == '5', valueOf returns 10 (not used as fallback to toString)

gradingCriteria.answerAccuracy: Must get all 7 outputs correct, especially the valueOf vs toString priority
gradingCriteria.explanationDepth: Should explain the valueOf/toString preference order and why String(obj2) returns 'hello' not '[object Object]'
